ground_truth.title: Fix It Friday: Struggling With How To Arrange Your Living Room? We Help 4 Readers Find Solutions (Including One Universal Trick That 9/10 Works)
ground_truth.author: Arlyn Hernandez
ground_truth.main_content:
Figuring out how to lay out the furniture in your home is a funny thing. When you see a room that has a great, functional layout, it looks so easy. But then you step into a blanket space, have a grab bag of furniture to use, and you freeze. It can be HARD. There are many right ways in any given room to arrange pieces, but there are also plenty of wrong ways. And by wrong, I just mean not best suited to maximize your space, your seating, and even your square footage. Today, I'm helping four readers on the layout struggle bus because sometimes, you've gotta call in the reinforcements. And friends, after reading their submissions (and dozens of others), I started to realize there was a common thread throughout many homes.

The universal tip that emerges repeatedly is: Take your furniture away from the walls, especially your sofa. Yes, sometimes a sofa or sectional is great up against the wall. There's nothing wrong with it AT ALL. But if your room doesn't feel like it's working and all your big pieces are around the perimeter of your space, GET THEM IN THE CENTER OF THE ROOM. Our rooms need to breathe. When everything lines the walls, it can strangle a room while also leaving way too much open space in the center.

For Erin H., who has a living room backing up to a kitchen island, the solution involves shifting the sofa to face the fireplace at the edge of the rug, moving chairs to the opposite side closest to the fireplace and built-ins, and adding storage on the wall where the current sofa is. The space is small, but this arrangement leaves room in front of the sliding glass doors to move around.

For Liz W., dealing with a large 16'x15' room that feels both too small and too large, the advice is to turn the rug 90 degrees so its width follows the width of the room, move the sofa off the window to face the fireplace, place one of each new chair on either side of the rug, and add a thin plant table in front of the window.

For Lucy C., whose living room contains the front door and connects to the dining room, the sofa should be pulled away from the wall to be parallel with the fireplace. The rug should be centered on the fireplace, and the green armchair should be moved to improve flow from the front door.

For Jenna S., struggling with an L-shaped living room for 15 years, the advice is to treat it like two different spaces. Move the seating down into the room and create something else where space opens up. Consider either a long 100-inch or more sofa, or a proper L-shaped sectional, and move the TV from the corner onto the flat wall.
with: TAKE YOUR FURNITURE AWAY FROM THE WALLS!!!! Especially your sofa.
Our rooms need to breathe. When everything lines the walls, it can strangle a room while also leaving waaaaay too much open space in the center.
I know this reader likely put the sofa where she put it because they thought placing it in front of the dining room entryway would block the flow of traffic.
When you have a strange L-shaped room, treat it like two different spaces. Suddenly, it's not an L, it's two blocks of space, instead.
You have got to leave room for air; for nothing; for visual boredom.
